title: Optical Illusion Detection using ConvLSTM and Optical Flow Networks
skills: Python, PyTorch, TensorFlow, ConvLSTM, FlowNet2, OpenCV, Computer Vision, Time Series Modeling, Statistical Testing
details: Developed a deep learning pipeline for detecting optical illusions from video sequences using ConvLSTM and optical flow architectures. Preprocessed high-resolution first-person video data (~529k frames at ~30 FPS), extracting and normalizing frames to 120x120 grayscale format. Reduced dataset size by ~30% using variance-based frame filtering. Implemented ConvLSTM to model spatio-temporal dependencies and predict future frames; evaluated prediction accuracy using Mean Absolute Error (MAE=0.189) and error rate (~7.4%). Conducted statistical hypothesis testing (t-statistic=30.44, p<1e-180) confirming significant deviation between predicted and actual frames. Extended pipeline with NVIDIA FlowNet2 for optical flow estimation, generating motion vector fields (120x120x2) to capture pixel-level movement dynamics. Demonstrated effectiveness of hybrid temporal + motion-based modeling for illusion detection and dynamic scene understanding.

title: Low-Order Neural System Modeling of C. elegans using RNNs
skills: Python, TensorFlow, PyTorch, LSTM, GRU, Time Series Modeling, Computational Neuroscience, Simulation Modeling, RMSE Evaluation
details: Developed recurrent neural network models (GRU, LSTM, Simple RNN) to approximate the neural dynamics of the C. elegans nervous system as a low-order surrogate for computationally expensive NEURON simulations. Trained models on multi-timescale simulation data (0.1 ms vs 0.5 ms resolution), demonstrating stable convergence and comparable loss across resolutions. Achieved low prediction error (RMSE ~0.023Đ0.043 across configurations) and identified GRU (16 hidden units) as optimal trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency. Conducted input ablation experiments showing neuron-specific dependencies (e.g., AVB _ LUAL/PVR, PLM _ DB1/VB1), improving interpretability of neural pathways. Extended experiments to multi-behavior datasets (FCM + Nictation) with 28 output neurons, demonstrating scalability and generalization. Established that low-dimensional RNNs outperform high-complexity models, reducing overfitting while preserving predictive fidelity.

title: Deep Learning for Biological and Visual Systems Modeling
skills: Python, PyTorch, TensorFlow, ConvLSTM, GRU, LSTM, Computer Vision, Computational Neuroscience, Time Series Modeling
details: Conducted multi-domain research applying deep learning to biological systems and visual perception tasks. Developed ConvLSTM-based pipelines for optical illusion detection using large-scale video data (~500k+ frames), integrating optical flow (FlowNet2) for motion estimation and achieving statistically significant prediction accuracy (MAE=0.189, p<1e-180). Built recurrent neural network models (GRU, LSTM) to approximate neural dynamics of the C. elegans nervous system, achieving low RMSE (~0.02Đ0.04) and demonstrating strong generalization across behaviors and time resolutions. Performed ablation-based interpretability analysis to identify neuron-level dependencies and reduce model complexity, showing that low-order models outperform high-dimensional architectures while maintaining predictive performance.

title: Microbiome Community Profiling and Rarefaction Analysis
skills: R, phyloseq, microshades, ggplot2, dplyr, tidyverse, cowplot, patchwork, microbiome analysis, ecological data visualization, compositional data analysis, rarefaction, taxonomy profiling, unsupervised learning, DBSCAN, t-SNE, autoencoders, TensorFlow, Keras
details: Analyzed microbiome community composition using the GlobalPatterns phyloseq dataset and R-based microbial ecology workflows. Built reproducible pipelines using phyloseq, microshades, ggplot2, dplyr, tidyverse, cowplot, patchwork, and forcats to preprocess microbiome abundance data, remove missing samples, agglomerate taxa, normalize abundances, and generate publication-style compositional visualizations. Focused on major bacterial phyla including Verrucomicrobia, Proteobacteria, Actinobacteria, Bacteroidetes, and Firmicutes, with genus-level breakdowns across sample types. Produced stacked abundance plots and faceted sample-type comparisons for freshwater, freshwater creek, ocean, soil, sediment, skin, feces, and tongue microbiome samples. Subsetted the full dataset into water, land, and human-associated microbiome groups and generated separate taxonomic contribution plots to compare mean, median, and distributional abundance patterns across ecological environments. Identified sample-type differences in microbial composition, including strong contrasts across freshwater, creek, and ocean communities. Extended the project scope from exploratory taxonomic profiling toward machine-learning-based microbiome representation learning by applying autoencoders for normalization and latent embedding generation, improving downstream clustering and classification of microbial communities. Used unsupervised learning methods including DBSCAN and t-SNE to detect rare microbial patterns and low-dimensional structure, supporting identification of uncommon bacterial strains and improving classification accuracy by approximately 17% over conventional preprocessing approaches. This work strengthened experience in biological data science, compositional data analysis, microbiome visualization, dimensionality reduction, and interpretable unsupervised learning for ecological and biomedical datasets.

title: Executive Function Task Automation: Abstract and Ecological Shifting Paradigms
skills: Python, PsychoPy, pandas, experimental design, behavioral task programming, reaction time analysis, cognitive control, task-switching, response accuracy, ANOVA, mixed-effects modeling, data validation
details: Developed and automated PsychoPy-based executive-function shifting tasks to measure cognitive flexibility, task-switching cost, response accuracy, and reaction time across abstract and ecological stimulus conditions. Built a full experimental workflow including participant ID collection, instruction screens, practice blocks, repeatable practice loops, randomized trial generation, task cues, stimulus presentation, response collection, timing capture in milliseconds, feedback during practice, break screens, and participant-level CSV export. Implemented an abstract shifting paradigm requiring participants to switch between shape-based and color-based matching rules using circles, triangles, blue/yellow colors, and left/right response mappings. Implemented an ecological shifting paradigm requiring participants to switch between gender and emotion classification using face stimuli, gender icons, and emotion icons. Each main shifting task included 66 randomized trials split into two 33-trial blocks with a midpoint break. Trial-level outputs included trial number, question/rule type, stimulus features, participant response, response time, correctness, and trial type classified as first, repeat, or switch. Designed the data structure to support downstream statistical analysis of switch cost using repeated-measures ANOVA or mixed-effects models comparing response time and accuracy across switch vs. repeat trials, abstract vs. ecological stimuli, and rule domains. The task infrastructure enabled analysis of cognitive flexibility, processing speed, inhibition, stimulus-rule mapping, and ecological validity in behavioral experiments.

title: Executive Function Task Automation: Abstract and Ecological Updating Paradigms
skills: Python, PsychoPy, pandas, working memory, updating task design, behavioral data pipelines, response-time measurement, accuracy scoring, ANOVA, repeated-measures analysis, experimental psychology
details: Built PsychoPy-based working-memory updating tasks to measure participantsŐ ability to maintain and update mental counts across sequentially presented stimuli. Developed both abstract and ecological versions of the task. The abstract version presented sequences of geometric stimuli including circles, squares, and triangles, while the ecological version presented real-world objects including faces, bills, and buses. Each task included instruction screens, worked examples, practice trials with feedback, optional repeated practice, five main experimental rounds, randomized stimulus sequences, response collection through visual radio-button interfaces, and automated participant-level CSV export. Practice trials used 5-item sequences with feedback, while the main task used 5 rounds of 9-item sequences. Recorded response time for each round, actual counts for each stimulus category, participant-reported counts, and category-specific correctness indicators. The resulting dataset supports trial- and participant-level analyses of working-memory updating accuracy, response latency, counting error, object-domain effects, and abstract versus ecological stimulus differences. Designed the output structure for repeated-measures ANOVA and mixed-effects modeling, including comparisons of response time and accuracy across stimulus domain, category type, task condition, and round. This work improved experimental reproducibility, reduced manual administration burden, and created a standardized behavioral-data pipeline for cognitive psychology research.

title: Deep Learning and Statistical Quality Control for Casting Defect Detection
skills: Python, TensorFlow, Keras, CNN, Image Processing, Statistical Quality Control, NP Chart, C Chart, Control Charts, Data Augmentation, Industrial Analytics
details: Developed an end-to-end pipeline combining deep learning-based defect detection with statistical quality control (SQC) methods for industrial casting inspection. Collected and processed a labeled dataset of 1,039 casting images (defective vs. non-defective) sourced from Kaggle, including multiple defect categories such as blowholes, pinholes, shrinkage, burrs, and metallurgical defects. Preprocessed images by converting to grayscale and resizing to 300_300_1 resolution, followed by extensive data augmentation including rotation (±40ˇ), shear (0.2), height/width shifts (0.2), and horizontal flipping to improve generalization. Implemented a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture consisting of three Conv2D layers with MaxPooling, trained using RMSprop optimizer (learning rate = 1e-5) and binary cross-entropy loss over 25 epochs. Achieved validation accuracy of 76.63% and loss of 0.5114 on the test set. Deployed the trained model on an unseen validation batch of 200 casting images, generating defect classification outputs used for downstream statistical process monitoring. Constructed Statistical Quality Control charts including NP chart and C chart using model-predicted defect counts across 20 subgroups (sample size = 10 per subgroup). Computed control limits: NP chart with center line (p_ Ĺ 0.60), UCL Ĺ 10.65, LCL Ĺ 1.35; C chart with __ Ĺ 6.0, UCL Ĺ 13.35, LCL Ĺ -1.35. Empirically demonstrated that all subgroup defect counts fell within control limits, indicating the manufacturing process was statistically in control under model-based inspection. Integrated machine learning predictions with classical SQC frameworks, demonstrating a hybrid methodology for automated industrial quality monitoring, defect detection, and process stability assessment.
